ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301737 'lemon drink' is the closest parent; no exact FoodOn term exists for lemon syrup cordial as served in Malaysian/Singaporean context.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lemon-flavoured soft drink / cordial (closest parent category in FoodEx2 non-alcoholic beverages)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest parent for lemon-flavoured sweetened beverage; no exact FoodEx2 code for Malaysian-style sirup lemon cordial.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 'Fruit drink' is the closest defensible parent; no exact SNOMED CT code for lemon syrup cordial.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of lemon syrup drink, approximately 250 ml, as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean kopitiam or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon cordial / lemon squash, ready-to-drink — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14148 / analogous lemon-flavoured sweetened beverage)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lemon-flavoured sweetened beverage / lemon cordial (ready-to-drink dilution). Malaysian sirup lemon is a sugar-lemon syrup diluted with water; values reflect a typical ready-to-drink dilution at approximately 10–12% sugar concentration, consistent with USDA lemon drink data. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not contain a specific sirup lemon entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this beverage category.

gi_source.title: Fructose, glucose, and sucrose solutions and lemon-flavoured sweetened beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian sirup lemon. GI of 63 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sucrose solution / lemon-flavoured sweetened beverage (GI ~63, medium category), which is the closest analogous food. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (63 × 10.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 16.6. This is consistent with peer-reviewed GI tables for sugar-sweetened lemon drinks.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 g); no significant sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Sirup lemon is very low in sodium and does not meaningfully contribute to blood pressure elevation; however, its high sugar content may contribute to weight gain, which is an indirect risk factor for hypertension, so moderate consumption is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (~10.2 g per 100 g) and medium glycemic index (~63) leading to rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid sirup lemon due to its high free sugar content and medium GI, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 25.5 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~16.6.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as sirup lemon is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional consumption and prefer unsweetened alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sirup Lemon (Lemon Syrup Drink) is a popular chilled sweetened beverage widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore, commonly served at kopitiams, mamak stalls, and casual restaurants. It is prepared by diluting a concentrated lemon-flavoured sugar syrup with chilled water or served over ice, resulting in a bright, sweet, and tangy lemon-flavoured drink. The texture is thin and watery with a pronounced sweetness balanced by mild citrus acidity. The drink is essentially a sugar-water solution flavoured with lemon juice or lemon flavouring and citric acid, making it a high-sugar, low-nutrient beverage. Per 100 g ready-to-drink, it provides approximately 40 kcal, 10.5 g carbohydrate (predominantly free sugars ~10.2 g), negligible protein and fat, and very low sodium (~5 mg). It contains a small amount of vitamin C (~3 mg per 100 g) from lemon content. Clinically, sirup lemon is of high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium glycemic index (~63) and high free sugar load; a standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 25.5 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~16.6. It is also of moderate concern for cardiovascular health due to the association between regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption and elevated triglycerides. The drink is naturally free of fat, protein, dietary fibre, and cholesterol, and is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diets. It is not recommended as a regular beverage for individuals managing blood glucose or metabolic syndrome.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemon syrup drink?
What is the sugar content of lemon cordial?
What is the GI of lemon syrup drink?
Is sirup lemon suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Malaysian sirup lemon exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lemon-flavoured sweetened beverages at typical ready-to-drink dilution. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous sucrose-based lemon drink. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is well-supported and the food is compositionally simple, but no exact primary regional source match was found.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: sirup lemon is a sugar-water beverage containing no protein by composition. 2. fat_g is naturally zero: this beverage contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous lemon drink data. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are all 0.0 — naturally zero for a sugar-water lemon beverage; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this beverage category. 5. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 6. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 7. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 12. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 13. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 14. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 15. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 16. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 17. Input food_group was 'Grains and staples' which is incorrect for a beverage; corrected to 'Beverages'. 18. Input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' and 'Dosa' which are irrelevant to this beverage; these are artefacts of the field survey seed data and have been disregarded. 19. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for sucrose-based lemon-flavoured sweetened beverage; no direct GI measurement for Malaysian sirup lemon was found in approved sources. 20. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (63 × 10.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 16.6.
